kw_02: Trading in securities when in possession of unpublished price sensitive information is prohibited under Regulation 3 and 4 of SEBI PIT Regulations 2015. The regulations designate such conduct as "insider trading laws" under Section 15G of the SEBI Act. **Note:** Specific penalty amounts (₹25 crore or 3× profit, whichever is higher) are in Section 15G of the SEBI Act, which is referenced but not reproduced in the PIT regulations text.
kw_04: Under Regulation 3(1) of SEBI SAST 2011, a mandatory open offer is triggered when an acquirer, together with persons acting in concert, acquires shares or voting rights that would entitle them to exercise 25% or more of the voting rights in the target company. Under Regulation 3(2), an acquirer already holding 25% or more must make an open offer if they acquire more than 5% additional voting rights in any financial year. Regulation 4 additionally requires an open offer upon acquisition of control over the target company regardless of shareholding level.
kw_06: The provided text confirms that the main body of the regulations does not explicitly state a minimum corpus figure; rather, Regulation 35(1) states that the asset management company shall specify the "minimum subscription amount it seeks to raise" in the offer document. However, the claim regarding the 1% or ₹50 lakh investment requirement under Regulation 28 is outdated according to the corpus; the text notes that sub-regulation (4) containing this rule was omitted by the SEBI (Mutual Funds) (Second Amendment) Regulations, 2021. Furthermore, the specific figures of ₹20 crore and ₹10 crore are not present in the provided text.
kw_07: Under the SEBI PIT Regulations 2015, a designated person who formulates a trading plan must observe a cool-off period of 120 calendar days from the date of public disclosure of the trading plan before the first trade under the plan can be executed. This is because companies declare results quarterly and trading restrictions typically exist for about one month per quarter, making 120 days sufficient for UPSI in possession at plan formulation to become generally available. The trading window re-opens not earlier than 48 hours after price-sensitive information becomes generally available.
kw_09: Under Regulation 25 of SEBI ICDR 2018, an issuer must file three copies of the draft red herring prospectus (DRHP) with SEBI through the lead manager(s) prior to making a public offer, along with requisite fees. The DRHP must also be filed simultaneously with the stock exchanges where securities are proposed to be listed. SEBI then has 30 days from the later of: (a) receipt of the DRHP, (b) receipt of satisfactory replies to queries, or (c) receipt of clarification from any regulator, to issue its observations. The issuer must incorporate SEBI's observations before opening the issue.
kw_10: Under Regulation 18(1) of SEBI LODR 2015, every listed entity must constitute a qualified and independent audit committee with the following requirements: (a) minimum three directors as members; (b) two-thirds of members must be independent directors; (c) all members must be financially literate (able to read and understand basic financial statements); and (d) at least one member must have accounting or related financial management expertise. The chairperson of the audit committee must be an independent director.
kw_11: Under Regulation 16 of SEBI ICDR 2018: (a) The minimum promoters' contribution (minimum 20% of post-issue capital) must be locked in for 18 months from the date of allotment in the IPO (extended to 3 years if the majority of issue proceeds are proposed to be utilised for capital expenditure); (b) Promoters' holding in excess of the minimum promoters' contribution must be locked in for 6 months from the date of allotment (extended to 1 year if majority proceeds are for capital expenditure).
kw_13: Under Regulation 34(1) of SEBI LODR 2015, a listed entity shall submit the annual report to the stock exchange within 21 working days of it being approved and adopted in the Annual General Meeting, as per the provisions of the Companies Act, 2013. Additionally, under Regulation 33, the annual audited standalone financial results must be submitted within 60 days from the end of the financial year.
sem_01: Under SEBI ICDR 2018, an issuer can raise capital through methods other than a public issue, including: (1) Qualified Institutions Placement (QIP) under Chapter VI, which is an issue of eligible securities to qualified institutional buyers on a private placement basis; (2) Rights Issue under Chapter III, which is an offer to existing shareholders as on a fixed record date; and (3) Preferential Issue under Chapter V, which is an issue to select persons on a private placement basis subject to specific pricing and lock-in norms. To undertake a QIP, the company's equity shares of the same class must have been listed on a stock exchange for a period of at least one year prior to the notice issuance.
sem_02: Under SEBI SAST 2011: when an acquirer reaches or crosses 25% voting rights, they must make a public announcement of an open offer for at least 26% of the total shares of the target company. The target company's board must, upon receipt of the detailed public statement, constitute a committee of independent directors to provide reasoned recommendations on the open offer to shareholders. The acquirer's obligations include appointing a manager to the open offer, making firm financial arrangements, publishing detailed public statements, and completing the open offer within the prescribed timeline.
sem_03: Under SEBI PIT Regulations 2015: (1) The company must maintain a structured digital database (with time-stamping and audit trails) recording all persons who share and receive UPSI (Regulation 3(5), p14); (2) A trading window must be maintained — closed when the compliance officer determines that designated persons can reasonably be expected to have UPSI (Schedule B, para 4, p62); (3) The company must establish a Chinese wall between persons handling UPSI and others; (4) Designated persons and their immediate relatives must not trade when the trading window is closed; (5) The structured digital database must be preserved for at least 8 years (p15).
sem_04: Under SEBI SAST 2011: (1) The acquirer must make an open offer for at least 26% of total shares of the target company (Regulation 7), giving all public shareholders the right to exit; (2) The open offer price must be the highest of several parameters, including the price agreed with promoters, and the VWAP price for 52 weeks, 26 weeks, or 60 trading days preceding the announcement (Regulation 8); (3) The independent committee of the target board must provide written reasoned recommendations to shareholders on whether to accept the offer (Regulation 26); (4) Shareholders are entitled to 10% p.a. interest on delayed payment if the acquirer fails to complete the offer on time; (5) The acquirer can seek to delist the target company through the open offer process if they declare their intention to do so upfront (Regulation 5A).
sem_05: Under SEBI Mutual Funds Regulations 1996: (1) Trustees are the primary protectors — they oversee the AMC and can dismiss the AMC with SEBI approval (Fourth Schedule); (2) The AMC must adhere to a Code of Conduct for Fund Managers and Dealers (Fifth Schedule, Part B), which restricts unethical activities; (3) The AMC must have an institutional mechanism to deter market abuse, including front-running and fraudulent transactions (Regulation 25); (4) Separate fund managers must be appointed for each fund unless investment objectives and portfolios are replicated (Regulation 24); (5) Trustees must quarterly review all related party transactions between the mutual fund, AMC, and its associates (Regulation 18); (6) SEBI inspects AMCs and can impose penalties, suspend, or cancel registration for violations (Chapters VIII & IX).
sem_06: Under SEBI LODR 2015 (Regulation 6), the compliance officer (who must be a qualified company secretary) is responsible for ensuring conformity with regulatory provisions, coordinating with SEBI and stock exchanges, ensuring correct procedures for filings, and monitoring the grievance redressal email. Additionally, under the SEBI PIT Regulations, the compliance officer is responsible for administering the insider trading code of conduct, determining trading window closures, and approving/monitoring trading plans and pre-clearances for designated persons.
sem_08: Under SEBI regulations: (1) Under SEBI PIT Regulation 7(2), a promoter must disclose to the company any acquisition or disposal of shares exceeding ₹10 lakh in a calendar quarter within 2 trading days; (2) Under SEBI SAST Regulation 29, a promoter must disclose changes in shareholding exceeding 2% (if they already hold 5% or more) within 2 working days; (3) If selling to an acquirer and it crosses the 25% threshold, an open offer obligation arises for the acquirer under SAST; (4) Promoter shares subject to minimum contribution lock-in under SEBI ICDR cannot be sold during the 18-month or 3-year lock-in period; (5) Under SEBI PIT, the promoter must comply with trading window and pre-clearance requirements before trading.
sem_09: Under SEBI ICDR 2018: (1) Meet eligibility criteria under Regulation 6 (net tangible assets ≥₹3 crore, average operating profit of at least ₹15 crore during the preceding 3 years, and net worth ≥₹1 crore); (2) Appoint lead manager(s), syndicate members, registrar, and a compliance officer; (3) File the draft offer document with SEBI and stock exchanges; (4) Receive SEBI observations within 30 days of filing/replies; (5) Make public issue and allot shares utilizing the ASBA facility; (6) Submit a final post-issue report to SEBI within 7 days of finalizing the basis of allotment; (7) Apply for and receive listing and trading permission from stock exchanges.
sem_10: Under Regulation 23 of SEBI LODR 2015: (1) All related party transactions (RPTs) require the prior approval of the audit committee; (2) A transaction is considered "material" if it exceeds 10% of the annual consolidated turnover of the listed entity, and all material RPTs require approval of the shareholders through a resolution; (3) All entities falling under the definition of related parties shall abstain from voting on material RPT resolutions; (4) The company must formulate a policy on the materiality of related party transactions; (5) The audit committee must review the details of RPTs entered into pursuant to omnibus approvals at least on a quarterly basis.
sem_11: Under SEBI PIT Regulations 2015: (1) Every key managerial personnel and director must disclose their holding of securities to the company within 7 days of their appointment (Regulation 7(1)); (2) Any designated person, promoter, or director must disclose to the company the number of securities acquired or disposed of within 2 trading days if the traded value exceeds ₹10 lakh over a calendar quarter (Regulation 7(2)); (3) The company must notify the stock exchange of such trading within 2 trading days of receipt; (4) Pre-clearance from the compliance officer is required for trades above a board-stipulated threshold when the trading window is open; (5) Off-market inter-se transfers between insiders are permitted as an exception if both parties possess the same UPSI and report it within 2 working days.
sem_13: Under Regulation 52 of SEBI Mutual Funds Regulations 1996, the AMC is entitled to charge certain expenses to a mutual fund scheme within the total expense ratio (TER) limits prescribed by SEBI. The TER is a percentage of daily net assets and is capped based on the AUM slab of the scheme (e.g., for equity-oriented schemes, 2.25% for the first ₹500 crore of daily net assets, which scales down progressively as AUM increases). Exit loads charged must be credited back to the scheme. Any expenditure in excess of the prescribed limits must be borne by the AMC, the trustees, or the sponsors.
mh_02: Under Regulation 2(1)(d) of SEBI PIT 2015: A "connected person" is (i) any person who is, or has been during the 6 months prior to the concerned act, associated with a company in any capacity directly or indirectly — including as an officer, employee, director, or otherwise having a relationship (temporary or permanent) allowing access to UPSI. Deemed connected persons include: (a) immediate relatives; (b) holding/subsidiary/associate companies; (c) SEBI-registered intermediaries and their employees; (d) mutual fund trustees, AMC directors and employees; (e) stock exchange officials; (f) bankers; (g) government officials who receive UPSI. Trading restrictions: connected persons and insiders must not trade when in possession of UPSI (Regulation 4). They must comply with trading window closures, obtain pre-clearance for trades above threshold, and disclose trades within 2 business days.
mh_03: Triggers (Regulation 3 and 4 of SEBI SAST 2011): (1) Acquiring shares/voting rights that take total holdings to 25% or more; (2) If already at 25%+, acquiring more than 5% additional in a financial year; (3) Acquiring direct or indirect control over the target company. Acquirer obligations upon trigger: (1) Make a public announcement immediately; (2) Ensure firm financial arrangements for the open offer; (3) Open offer must be for at least 26% of total shares; (4) Offer price must meet the minimum price formula; (5) Open offer must be kept open for 10 working days; (6) Payment to accepting shareholders must be made within 10 working days of closure; (7) Manager to the open offer must be appointed; (8) Escrow account must be opened for 25% of the consideration on the first ₹500 crore, and an additional 10% on the balance consideration.
mh_04: Eligibility (Regulation 61 of SEBI ICDR 2018): An issuer is ineligible to make a rights issue if the issuer, any of its promoters, promoter group, or directors are debarred from accessing the capital market by SEBI; if any of its promoters or directors is a fugitive economic offender; or if the equity shares of the issuer are suspended from trading as a disciplinary measure. If the issuer or a promoter/director is a wilful defaulter or fraudulent borrower, they are not strictly barred from launching a rights issue, but promoters/promoter groups shall not renounce their rights (Regulation 62). Letter of Offer disclosures must include all SEBI-prescribed disclosures under Part B of Schedule VI.
mh_05: Under Regulation 2(1)(n) of SEBI PIT 2015, "unpublished price sensitive information" (UPSI) means any information relating to a company or its securities that is not generally available and which upon becoming generally available, is likely to materially affect the price of the securities. Examples specified include: financial results, dividends, change in capital structure, mergers/acquisitions/demergers, changes in key managerial personnel, and regulatory actions. Consequences of trading on UPSI: Any contravention of these regulations shall be dealt with by SEBI in accordance with the Act (Regulation 10), which can include initiating actions under Section 11, 11B, or Section 24 of the SEBI Act, seeking disgorgement, or handling proceedings under the SEBI Settlement Proceedings Regulations (as referenced in Chapter IIIA for informants).
mh_07: Under Regulation 5A of SEBI SAST 2011, an acquirer making a public announcement of an open offer may simultaneously seek the delisting of the target company by making a delisting offer. This is allowed provided the acquirer declares their intention to delist upfront at the time of making the public announcement and the detailed public statement. If the delisting attempt fails (e.g., due to failing the threshold or not receiving shareholder approval), the acquirer must announce the failure within two working days and comply with all applicable provisions to complete the standard open offer.
mh_08: Under SEBI Mutual Funds Regulations 1996: NFO requirements include filing the offer document with SEBI before launching the scheme and paying the requisite filing fees (Regulation 28). The offer document must contain adequate disclosures to enable investors to make an informed decision, including the disclosure of maximum investments proposed to be made by the scheme in the listed securities of the group companies of the sponsor (Regulation 29). SEBI has 21 working days from the date of filing to suggest modifications; if no modifications are suggested, the asset management company may issue the offer document.
mh_09: Under Regulation 31 of SEBI SAST 2011 (not LODR), the promoter of every target company must disclose details of shares encumbered by him or by persons acting in concert with him. Any creation, invocation, or release of such encumbrance must be disclosed within 7 working days to every stock exchange where the shares are listed and to the target company at its registered office. Additionally, promoters must declare on a yearly basis to the stock exchange and the audit committee that they have not made any undisclosed encumbrances during the financial year.
mh_10: Under Regulation 10(1) of SEBI SAST 2011, the following acquisitions are exempt from the open offer obligation: (a) inter se transfers among qualifying persons (immediate relatives, promoters, group companies); (b) acquisition pursuant to a scheme under the Companies Act or BIFR; (c) acquisition pursuant to the Securitisation and Reconstruction of Financial Assets and Enforcement of Security Interest Act; (d) acquisition by way of transmission, succession, or inheritance. Disclosure obligations continue: for these exempt acquisitions, the acquirer must intimate the stock exchanges 4 working days prior to the proposed acquisition, file a report within 4 working days of the acquisition, and submit a detailed report to SEBI within 21 working days accompanied by a non-refundable fee of ₹1,50,000.
mh_11: Under Regulation 3(5) of SEBI PIT 2015: (1) The board of directors (or head of organization) of every person required to handle UPSI must ensure a structured digital database is maintained; (2) It must record: (a) the nature of UPSI shared; (b) names of persons who shared the UPSI; (c) names of persons with whom UPSI is shared; (d) Permanent Account Number (or other authorized identifier) of all such persons; (3) The database must NOT be outsourced — must be maintained internally; (4) Must have adequate internal controls: time-stamping and audit trails to ensure non-tampering; (5) Must be preserved for not less than 8 years after completion of the relevant transactions; (6) Upon notice of SEBI investigation, must be preserved until completion of proceedings. Responsibility: the board of directors or head of the organisation.
mh_12: Under SEBI Mutual Funds Regulations 1996: AMC governance standards: (1) Net worth of not less than ₹50 crore deployed in assets (Regulation 21(f)); (2) At least 50% of AMC directors must not be associated with the sponsor; (3) Chairman of AMC must not be a trustee of any mutual fund; (4) Must implement a code of conduct for fund managers and dealers; (5) Cannot act as a trustee of any mutual fund. Sponsor liability/requirements: (1) The sponsor must contribute at least 40% of the net worth of the AMC; (2) For sponsors failing the profitability track record, they must capitalize the AMC to ₹150 crore net worth, locked in for 5 years, and appoint experienced personnel with a combined experience of at least 30 years.
mh_13: Under Regulation 33 of SEBI LODR 2015: (1) Standalone quarterly and year-to-date financial results: within 45 days from the end of each quarter (other than the last quarter); (2) For the last quarter: audited standalone annual results within 60 days from the end of the financial year; (3) Consolidated financial results (quarterly, if opted): same timelines as standalone; (4) The results must be approved by the board of directors and signed by the MD/Executive Director; (5) Listed entities are also required to submit limited review/audit reports with each set of results. Other quarterly disclosures under LODR include the shareholding pattern (Regulation 31) within 21 days from the end of each quarter.
cp_01: Under SEBI SAST Regulation 29, any change in promoter shareholding exceeding 2% (if they already hold 5% or more) must be disclosed to the stock exchange within 2 working days. If the reduction triggers SAST obligations (e.g., an acquirer receiving 25% or more), the acquirer must make an open offer. Open market sale: The same SAST Regulation 29 disclosure applies. Additionally, the promoter, as a designated person, must comply with PIT trading window restrictions, pre-clearance rules, and ensure they are not trading while in possession of UPSI. If the stake is sold through an offer for sale during a QIP (Regulation 173 of ICDR), it must be done to achieve minimum public shareholding, with specific 12-week cooling-off periods before and after the issue.
cp_02: Rights Issue: Eligibility — issuer must not be debarred by SEBI, no promoter/director can be a fugitive economic offender, and shares must not be suspended (Reg 61). Pricing — the issue price is determined by the issuer with no SEBI-prescribed minimum pricing formula. Preferential Allotment: Eligibility — issuer must comply with SEBI conditions including a special resolution and dematerialization (Reg 160). Pricing — for frequently traded shares, it must be the higher of the 90 trading days' volume-weighted average price or the 10 trading days' VWAP preceding the relevant date (Regulation 164). Lock-in — 18 months for promoters (up to 20% of capital) and 6 months for non-promoters and excess promoter holdings (Regulation 167).
cp_04: Acquirer obligations: (1) Make public announcement (PA) immediately upon trigger; (2) Make detailed public statement (DPS) within 5 working days of PA; (3) File draft letter of offer with SEBI within 5 working days of the DPS; (4) Ensure firm financial arrangements; (5) Open escrow account with 25% on the first ₹500 crore and 10% on the balance; (6) Dispatch letter of offer; (7) Keep offer open for 10 working days; (8) Make payment within 10 working days of offer closure. Target board obligations: (1) Constitute an independent committee upon receipt of the DPS; (2) Provide written reasoned recommendations to shareholders at least 2 working days before the offer opens; (3) Ensure the business is conducted in the ordinary course and not take defensive measures (like alienating assets or issuing new shares) without shareholder approval by postal ballot. Note: The acquirer (not the target board) appoints the manager to the open offer.
cp_05: PIT General Framework (Regulation 3 and 4): Applies to all insiders and connected persons — prohibits trading when in possession of UPSI regardless of trading window status. This is the universal rule applicable to any entity. Listed Company Trading Window (Schedule B under Regulation 9(1)): A more specific compliance mechanism — the compliance officer uses a notional trading window for designated persons and their immediate relatives, closing it when UPSI is expected (e.g., from the end of every quarter till 48 hours after financial results are declared). The window re-opens not earlier than 48 hours after the UPSI becomes generally available. Pre-clearance from the compliance officer is required for all trades by designated persons when the window is open.
cp_06: Related Party Transaction (RPT) obligations under LODR Regulation 23: (1) Prior audit committee approval before entering the RPT; (2) If a material RPT (exceeding 10% of annual consolidated turnover), obtain shareholder approval by resolution; (3) All entities falling under the definition of related parties must abstain from voting. Material Event obligations under LODR Regulation 30: (1) Disclose the material event to the stock exchange as soon as reasonably possible and not later than 24 hours from the occurrence; (2) The company may also confirm or deny reported events or information on its own initiative. Combined: If the RPT itself constitutes a material event, both the RPT approval process and the 24-hour material event disclosure must be complied with.
cp_08: Under SEBI ICDR 2018: (1) File draft offer document with SEBI and stock exchanges (Regulation 25); (2) SEBI reviews and issues observations within 30 days (Regulation 25(4)); (3) Incorporate SEBI observations and file updated offer document; (4) File offer document with Registrar of Companies (ROC); (5) Open the issue — minimum 3 days / maximum 10 days subscription period; (6) Process applications through ASBA; (7) Finalise basis of allotment with designated stock exchange; (8) Initiate refunds/unblocking within 4 days of issue closure (Regulation 45); (9) Receive in-principle listing approval from stock exchange prior to opening; (10) Final listing and trading approval from stock exchange. Post-listing: appoint a monitoring agency if proceeds exceed ₹100 crore.
cp_09: Trustees: Under Regulation 18 and 25, trustees must exercise due diligence to ensure the AMC complies with all regulations. They must periodically review AMC operations, related party transactions, and net worth. Trustees can terminate the AMC's assignment with a majority vote or 75% unitholder approval. AMC: AMCs must adhere to the Code of Conduct, maintain net worth, and prevent market abuse/front-running. Consequences: Under Chapter IX (Action in case of default), if a mutual fund, AMC, or trustee violates regulations, fails to cooperate, or indulges in unfair trade practices, SEBI can deal with the contravention under the SEBI Act or the SEBI (Intermediaries) Regulations, which may include suspension, cancellation of registration, or other disciplinary actions.
cp_10: Under SEBI LODR Regulation 30 (material events), all three trigger immediate disclosures: (1) Change in management (e.g., appointment/resignation of MD, CFO, company secretary): disclose to stock exchange within 24 hours. Also disclose in the next board meeting outcome disclosure. (2) Material litigation (e.g., a lawsuit where claim exceeds certain threshold or could materially affect business): disclose as a material event within 24 hours; also disclose in the notes to financial statements, annual report, and investor presentation. (3) Revision in credit rating: disclose to stock exchange within 24 hours of receiving communication from rating agency. All three must also be disclosed prominently on the company's website and in the next annual report. If any of the three occur simultaneously, each event must be disclosed separately with its individual disclosures.
cp_11: Under SEBI LODR Regulation 98, SEBI and the respective stock exchanges can impose actions for contravention of the regulations. The actions stock exchanges can take include: (1) Imposition of fines; (2) Suspension of trading; and (3) Freezing of promoter/promoter group holding of designated securities in coordination with depositories. Procedural rights: The exact procedural appeal rights (like SAT or Supreme Court) are governed by the overarching SEBI Act, though within the LODR framework, actions are specified to be handled in accordance with circulars or guidelines issued by the Board.
cp_12: Mutual Funds (SEBI MF Regs): (1) AMC must redress investor grievances promptly but not later than 21 calendar days from the date of receipt (Regulation 60A); (2) Trustees must periodically review the investor complaints received and the redressal of the same by the AMC; (3) A compliance officer is responsible for grievance redressal. Listed Companies (SEBI LODR): (1) Must appoint a Registrar and Share Transfer Agent (RTA) or manage the facility in-house (Regulation 7); (2) Must constitute a Stakeholders Relationship Committee to look into the mechanism of grievance redressal (Regulation 20); (3) Must file a quarterly statement to the stock exchange detailing the number of pending, received, and disposed investor complaints (Regulation 13); (4) Must register on the SCORES platform.
cp_13: Under multiple SEBI regulations: Disclosures: (1) Initial shareholding disclosure to company within 7 days of appointment (PIT Reg 7(1)); (2) Quarterly shareholding pattern disclosure (LODR Reg 31); (3) Any pledge of shares must be disclosed within 7 working days (SAST Reg 31); (4) Any change in shareholding must be disclosed to the company within 2 trading days if the traded value exceeds ₹10 lakh in a calendar quarter (PIT Reg 7(2)); (5) The company must further disclose this to the stock exchange within 2 trading days. Trading restrictions: (1) Cannot trade when the trading window is closed (PIT Schedule B); (2) Must obtain pre-clearance for trades; (3) Cannot trade when in possession of UPSI. Corporate Governance: (1) Must ensure related party transactions are approved by the audit committee (LODR Reg 23); (2) The audit committee must be chaired by an independent director.
